content.text: An Architecture Decision Record without a managed lifecycle is just a dated document. The lifecycle — draft, review, approval, periodic reassessment, deprecation, supersession — is what makes ADRs useful over the 2–3 year lifespan of an enterprise GenAI system. GenAI projects need more active lifecycle management than conventional software projects because the underlying technology, provider offerings, and regulatory landscape change faster. A model selection ADR accepted in 2024 may be technically obsolete by 2025.

This sub-subtopic covers the governance processes that keep an ADR system healthy: who approves, what the review cadence means in practice, what triggers an out-of-cycle review, and how to deprecate and supersede decisions without losing the institutional memory they capture.

attrs.alt: ADR state machine: full lifecycle from Draft to Superseded

content.text: Author is writing; not yet open for stakeholder comment
Decision in force; immutable content record
Decision explicitly not taken; reason preserved for future reference
Decision still in force but a replacement ADR is in Proposed/Review state
Approval panel when new ADR is Proposed
Replaced by a newer accepted ADR; kept for audit trail
Approval panel when new ADR is Accepted

The immutability of Accepted ADRs is not a bureaucratic constraint — it is an audit trail guarantee. If an ADR's content can be silently edited after acceptance, you cannot reconstruct what was decided, by whom, and on what basis at any point in the past. This matters for regulatory audits and post-incident reviews.

Every ADR that reaches Accepted status must pass through a defined approval gate. The gate is not a bureaucratic formality — it is the moment at which the decision becomes an organisational commitment rather than a personal preference.
DPO (if data residency involved), Legal (if DPA terms change)
Security (if new attack surface), Cost controller
Principal Architect + Product Owner + Security Lead

A Proposed ADR must remain open for a minimum of 5 business days before it can move to Accepted. This window serves two purposes: it gives distributed team members time to review, and it creates a documented period during which objections were invited and either resolved or overridden.
All comments must be resolved before Acceptance — either by editing the ADR, adding a note explaining why the comment was not incorporated, or escalating to the approval panel for a decision.
A comment is 'resolved' when the commenter marks it as resolved, or when the approval panel overrides it with documented reasoning.
Anonymous or undocumented objections are not blocking — only named objections from stakeholders in scope can block Acceptance.
If a Proposed ADR has unresolved blocking objections after 10 business days, it is moved to Rejected with a note, and the decision process restarted with a revised approach.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
from enum import Enum

class ApprovalVote(str, Enum):
    APPROVE = "approve"
    REJECT = "reject"
    ABSTAIN = "abstain"

@dataclass
class Approver:
    name: str
    role: str
    required: bool
    vote: Optional[ApprovalVote] = None
    comment: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class ApprovalPanel:
    adr_id: str
    approvers: list[Approver]

    def required_approvers(self) -> list[Approver]:
        return [a for a in self.approvers if a.required]

    def has_quorum(self) -> bool:
        required = self.required_approvers()
        voted_approve = [a for a in required if a.vote == ApprovalVote.APPROVE]
        voted_reject = [a for a in required if a.vote == ApprovalVote.REJECT]
        # Quorum: all required approvers have voted, majority approved, none rejected
        all_voted = all(a.vote is not None for a in required)
        return (
            all_voted
            and len(voted_approve) >= len(required) // 2 + 1
            and len(voted_reject) == 0
        )

    def blocking_votes(self) -> list[Approver]:
        return [a for a in self.required_approvers() if a.vote == ApprovalVote.REJECT]

    def status_summary(self) -> str:
        required = self.required_approvers()
        voted = [a for a in required if a.vote is not None]
        return (
            f"ADR-{self.adr_id}: {len(voted)}/{len(required)} required approvers voted | "
            f"Quorum: {'YES' if self.has_quorum() else 'NO'} | "
            f"Blockers: {len(self.blocking_votes())}"
        )

# FNOL ADR-001 approval example
panel = ApprovalPanel(
    adr_id="001",
    approvers=[
        Approver("Alice Chen", "Principal Architect", required=True, vote=ApprovalVote.APPROVE),
        Approver("Bob Okafor", "Legal", required=True, vote=ApprovalVote.APPROVE,
                 comment="DPA confirmed; no training data clause verified"),
        Approver("Carol Diaz", "DPO", required=True, vote=ApprovalVote.APPROVE,
                 comment="West Europe region satisfies GDPR data residency"),
        Approver("David Park", "Product Owner", required=True, vote=ApprovalVote.APPROVE),
        Approver("Frank Nguyen", "CISO", required=False, vote=ApprovalVote.ABSTAIN,
                 comment="No new attack surface; no security concerns"),
    ]
)
print(panel.status_summary())
# ADR-001: 4/4 required approvers voted | Quorum: YES | Blockers: 0

Every Accepted ADR has a review date set at acceptance. On the review date, the ADR owner performs an assessment and documents one of three outcomes: no change needed, amendment required (triggers new ADR), or deprecation triggered (replacement ADR already in progress).

## ADR Periodic Review Assessment — [ADR-NNN] [Title]
**Review date:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Reviewer:** [name, role]
**Original acceptance date:** YYYY-MM-DD

### Quality Assumption Check
For each quality assumption documented in the ADR:
- [ ] Accuracy/quality metric: current value [X] vs target [Y] — [PASS / WARN / FAIL]
- [ ] Latency metric: current P95 [X ms] vs SLO [Y ms] — [PASS / WARN / FAIL]
- [ ] Cost metric: current cost/req [£X] vs ceiling [£Y] — [PASS / WARN / FAIL]
- [ ] Availability: provider SLA still [X]% — [PASS / WARN / FAIL]

### Technology Landscape Check
- [ ] Is the selected model version still available and supported? [YES / DEPRECATION NOTICE dated YYYY-MM-DD]
- [ ] Has a significantly better or cheaper alternative emerged? [YES / NO]
  If YES: describe alternative and estimated improvement: [...]
- [ ] Has the provider's pricing changed materially (>20%)? [YES / NO]
- [ ] Is a new model tier available that changes the routing assumptions? [YES / NO]

### Compliance and Governance Check
- [ ] Is the DPA still current and signed? [YES / NO — expires YYYY-MM-DD]
- [ ] Has the data residency situation changed (new provider regions, regulatory change)? [NO / YES — describe]
- [ ] Are there new regulatory requirements affecting this decision? [NO / YES — describe]

### Review Outcome
- [ ] NO CHANGE NEEDED — decision still valid; update review date to [YYYY-MM-DD]
- [ ] AMENDMENT — decision needs updating; create ADR-NNN to supersede
- [ ] DEPRECATION — replacement ADR already in progress (ADR-NNN, Proposed)
- [ ] URGENT REVIEW — quality assumption failed or compliance risk identified; page architecture team

The review checklist is completed and committed to the ADR repository as a review note. The note is appended to the ADR file (under a '## Review History' section) rather than replacing content — maintaining the immutable record of the original decision while documenting the ongoing assessment.
Scheduled reviews catch anticipated drift. Out-of-cycle triggers catch unexpected events that make a decision obsolete or unsafe before the next scheduled review.
Architecture team paged; review within 24 hours; decision may be temporarily suspended
Review within 2 weeks; replacement ADR Proposed before deprecation date - 8 weeks
New provider region opens that satisfies previously-blocking constraint
Review within 4 weeks; replacement ADR if better option now available
Model update changes output characteristics (even same version)
Re-run eval suite; review within 48 hours if score changes > 5%
DPO paged; ADR frozen (no implementation changes) pending legal review
Security incident traceable to architectural decision
ADR immediately suspended pending security review; replacement ADR required
Review routing and model tier decisions within 24 hours

When an out-of-cycle trigger is severe enough, the ADR may be temporarily suspended — meaning the decision it describes should not be relied upon until the review is complete. Suspension is not a lifecycle state (it is not on the state machine) but an operational flag.

# In the ADR file, add a suspension notice at the top:
# Do NOT edit the ADR content — add this notice above the title

> **SUSPENDED** — 2025-02-14
> Reason: Model update (2025-02-12) caused classification F1 to drop from 0.89 to 0.74
> on flood-claim category. Routing to gpt-4o-mini suspended for classification requests
> until review is complete. All requests temporarily routed to gpt-4o.
> Reviewing party: Alice Chen (Principal Architect)
> Expected resolution: 2025-02-17
> Incident ticket: INC-2024-0847

Deprecating an ADR is the most common lifecycle event for mature GenAI systems. It happens when a better decision is available but the old one is not yet wrong — the system still works under the old decision, but a replacement is in progress.
Author the replacement ADR (the superseding one) and move it to Proposed.
Update the original ADR's Status line to: 'Deprecated — see ADR-NNN (Proposed)'.
Add a note in the original ADR's Links section: 'To be superseded by ADR-NNN'.
Run both ADRs through normal approval. When ADR-NNN is Accepted, update the original to Superseded.
Update the ADR index README to reflect the new status of both ADRs.

Never delete a superseded ADR. Post-incident reviews, regulatory audits, and new team member onboarding all benefit from being able to read what decision was in force at a given point in time — and why it was replaced. Archive systems that discard old ADRs lose this institutional memory permanently.

# Maintaining ADR supersession chains in the README

## Supersession chain: Model Provider Selection

| Version | ADR | Decision | Status | Period in force |
|---------|-----|----------|--------|-----------------|
| v1 | ADR-001 | Azure OpenAI gpt-4o, West Europe | Superseded by ADR-023 | 2024-11-15 – 2025-06-01 |
| v2 | ADR-023 | Azure OpenAI gpt-4o-2025-03, West + North Europe | Superseded by ADR-041 | 2025-06-01 – 2025-10-15 |
| v3 | ADR-041 | Azure OpenAI o3-mini for triage, gpt-4o for drafting | Accepted (current) | 2025-10-15 – present |

Reading this chain, a new team member can see:
- What model was in use at any past date
- What drove each change (linked in each ADR's Context section)
- Whether a compliance decision (region change, DPA update) coincided with a model change
Manual tracking of ADR lifecycle state across a large repository is error-prone. Lightweight tooling helps.

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Optional
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class ADRSummary:
    adr_id: str
    title: str
    status: str
    acceptance_date: Optional[date]
    review_date: Optional[date]
    superseded_by: Optional[str]
    path: Path

    def is_overdue_for_review(self, today: date) -> bool:
        if self.status != "Accepted":
            return False
        if self.review_date is None:
            return True   # no review date set = overdue
        return self.review_date < today

    def days_until_review(self, today: date) -> Optional[int]:
        if self.review_date is None:
            return None
        return (self.review_date - today).days

def parse_adr(path: Path) -> ADRSummary:
    text = path.read_text()
    # Extract status
    status_match = re.search(r"Status:\*\*\s+(\w+)", text)
    status = status_match.group(1) if status_match else "Unknown"
    # Extract date
    date_match = re.search(r"Date:\*\*\s+(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})", text)
    acc_date = date.fromisoformat(date_match.group(1)) if date_match else None
    # Extract review date
    review_match = re.search(r"Review date:\*\*\s+(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})", text)
    rev_date = date.fromisoformat(review_match.group(1)) if review_match else None
    # Extract superseded-by
    sup_match = re.search(r"Superseded by:\s+(ADR-\d+)", text)
    sup_by = sup_match.group(1) if sup_match else None
    # Extract title
    title_match = re.search(r"# ADR-(\d+):\s+(.+)", text)
    adr_id = title_match.group(1) if title_match else path.stem
    title = title_match.group(2).strip() if title_match else "Unknown"

    return ADRSummary(adr_id, title, status, acc_date, rev_date, sup_by, path)

def audit_adrs(adr_dir: Path, today: date) -> None:
    adrs = [parse_adr(p) for p in sorted(adr_dir.glob("ADR-*.md"))]
    overdue = [a for a in adrs if a.is_overdue_for_review(today)]
    upcoming = [a for a in adrs if a.days_until_review(today) is not None
                and 0 <= (a.days_until_review(today) or 999) <= 30]

    if overdue:
        print(f"OVERDUE FOR REVIEW ({len(overdue)}):")
        for a in overdue:
            print(f"  ADR-{a.adr_id}: {a.title} — review date was {a.review_date}")
    if upcoming:
        print(f"\nDUE FOR REVIEW IN 30 DAYS ({len(upcoming)}):")
        for a in upcoming:
            print(f"  ADR-{a.adr_id}: {a.title} — due {a.review_date} "
                  f"({a.days_until_review(today)} days)")

# Run as: python3 audit_adrs.py
# audit_adrs(Path("docs/adr"), date.today())

Run this audit script in CI on a weekly schedule and post results to the architecture team's Slack channel. An ADR that is overdue for review is not just a process violation — it is an indication that the team may be operating under an assumption that has not been deliberately confirmed.

The ADR lifecycle — Draft, Proposed, Accepted, Deprecated, Superseded, Rejected — gives GenAI architecture decisions a managed existence beyond their initial authoring. The approval process (defined panel composition, quorum rules, comment resolution) makes acceptance a genuine organisational commitment. The review cadence (scheduled by ADR type, triggered by production events) ensures decisions remain current in a technology landscape that changes faster than conventional software architecture.

Deprecation and supersession preserve institutional memory by keeping old decisions accessible and explaining why they were replaced. Automated lifecycle tooling — parsing ADR metadata, alerting on overdue reviews — prevents the process from rotting through inattention. Together, these mechanisms transform ADRs from documentation artifacts into a living governance system for GenAI architecture.
